name: Hine-nui-te-po
description: Hine-nui-te-po, in Māori mythology, holds the profoundly significant role of goddess of death and the underworld. She is not merely a personification of death but a powerful ancestral figure, representing the ultimate destination for all humankind. Originally known as Hine-ahu-one, the first woman fashioned from earth by Tāne, she fled to the underworld after discovering that her husband, Tāne, was also her father. This realization triggered a profound sense of shame and a desire to escape the world of light. In the underworld, she transformed into Hine-nui-te-po, assuming the role of guardian and receiver of the spirits of the deceased. Her body is often depicted as a doorway or portal, a powerful symbol of transition and the cyclical nature of life and death. She is a figure of both fear and reverence, embodying the inevitable end that awaits all living beings. Her presence underscores the Māori understanding of death as an integral part of the cosmic order, not simply an ending but a transformation.
subtitle: Goddess of Death

keyMyths.title: The Discovery of Incest and Flight to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Originally known as Hine-ahu-one, she lived happily with Tāne, the creator of humankind. However, she tragically discovered that Tāne was also her father, a result of his creation of her from the earth. Overwhelmed by shame and despair at this incestuous relationship, she fled from the world of light. Her journey led her to Te Reinga, the leaping-off place for spirits, where she transformed into Hine-nui-te-po, vowing to drag her descendants down with her into the realm of darkness.
keyMyths.source: Grey, Sir George. *Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race*. John Murray, 1854.

keyMyths.title: Māui's Failed Attempt to Conquer Death
keyMyths.description: The demigod Māui, seeking to grant immortality to humankind, attempted to enter Hine-nui-te-po's body through her vagina and exit through her mouth, thereby reversing the cycle of birth and death. However, his companions, witnessing this audacious act, burst into laughter. This disturbance awakened Hine-nui-te-po, who crushed Māui with the obsidian teeth in her vagina, thus sealing the fate of humanity and making death inevitable. This myth explains the origin of death and the futility of trying to overcome it.
keyMyths.source: Biggs, Bruce. *Māori Myths and Legends*. Reed, 1981.

keyMyths.title: The Role of Hine-nui-te-po in the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: Hine-nui-te-po is not merely a passive recipient of the dead. She actively guards and guides the spirits of the deceased in the underworld. She embodies the transition between the world of the living and the realm of the dead, ensuring that the spirits are properly received and integrated into the ancestral domain. The underworld is not necessarily a place of torment but rather a place of rest and reunion with ancestors, overseen by Hine-nui-te-po. Her presence provides comfort and guidance to those who have passed on.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. *The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend*. Canterbury University Press, 1995.
